---
title: "ai-minerals"
---

```{=html}
<object data="data/derived/portfolio_charts/region_locator_map.svg" type="image/svg+xml" style="width:100%; max-width:1100px; display:block; margin:0 auto;" aria-label="Map of the project regions across North America. Each dot links to its chapter.">
  <img src="data/derived/portfolio_charts/region_locator_map.svg" alt="Map of project regions; dots are clickable in modern browsers." style="width:100%;">
</object>
```

I've spent nearly thirty years building anti-malware on Windows:
kernel drivers, Windows internals, low-level systems work. None of
that has anything to do with mining; this site is what I've been
playing with in my off-hours.

A few years ago I started helping my mom research a book about her
grandfather, Walter W. Johnson, a mining and mechanical engineer and
builder of large placer-mining machinery. In the process we
discovered that the family still owned a large interest in several
patented mining claims in Nome, Alaska. One of the claims had
recently been high-graded; the others purportedly still held unmined
gold. As I started researching the book and the mining claims, two
things happened that I was not expecting. 1) I fell in love with
mining. 2) Every mining problem I encountered looked like it could
be decomposed into a computer program. Computing Bear Cub's resource
estimates from 100-year-old drill logs is one example of that.

The rest of this site is me poking around to see how useful public
data is for machine learning at the scale of an individual
prospector or a family-owned claim. One offshoot of that line of
investigation is goldbug, which unites it with one of my other
hobbies: title research. goldbug pairs the ML signal with public
land law: it ranks the parcels you can legally stake, not the ones
already withdrawn, patented, or under active claim.

## Chapters

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12}
### [1. Bear Cub (Cape Nome placer Au)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
[![1919 drill log from the Frozen Ground report: one of the 24 churn-drill records I OCRed](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch1_bear_cub_log.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
[![3D bedrock + pay-zone model rendered with PyVista from the 24 drill holes](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch1_bear_cub_3d.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)

[![Next-drill-site recommender: where the GP picks hole 25](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch1_bear_cub_gp.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)
:::
::::

A family-owned patented mining claim on the Third Beach Line at
Nome, staked in 1899 and last drilled in the 1910s-30s. I tracked
the 24 churn-drill logs down at the Huntington Library, built a
multi-engine OCR pipeline (Tesseract, TrOCR, Cloud Vision, Claude
Opus arbitrating with structured-output JSON) to ingest them,
mapped each hole onto a recent plat, built a 3D model of bedrock
and pay-zone, and computed Voronoi-cell volumetrics with Monte
Carlo uncertainty. Per-hole grades matched two mining engineers'
independent analyses within 6%. On top of the same grid, a
Gaussian-process recommender picks where hole #25 should go.
The OCR-cleaned records will export to [dh2loop](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/14/6711/2021/) format for the Loop3D 3D-modeling stack, and the recommender will be reframed as Efficacy of Information ([Caers et al. 2022](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11053-022-10030-1)).
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [2. Regional mineral-prospectivity pipelines](regional.qmd)

For each of the four regions (Eastern Alaska porphyry-Cu, BC Golden
Triangle multi-deposit, California Mother Lode orogenic-Au, Arizona
porphyry-Cu), I built the same kind of pipeline: pull public data
from the US Geological Survey, Natural Resources Canada, and state
surveys onto a common grid, train a Random Forest (a tree-ensemble
classifier that votes across hundreds of decision trees), and hold
out spatial blocks during validation so the model can't cheat by
memorizing neighbors. The two porphyry-Cu regions (Tanacross and
Arizona) both land at roughly 22 to 23 percent top-1% capture on
their held-out positives, the same regime despite a continent
between them.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![Tanacross (eastern Alaska) porphyry-Cu prospectivity, random forest output](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch2_regional.png)](regional.qmd)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [3. goldbug (live tool, CA Mother Lode)](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/goldbug.html)

The Mother Lode model from Chapter 2 is interesting on its own,
but it doesn't tell a prospector where to actually go. goldbug
closes that gap. It takes the per-cell probabilities and filters
them to federal land that's actually open for an individual
prospector to stake a claim on (the 1872 Mining Law calls this
filing a notice of location). The hard part isn't the modeling.
It's that the Bureau of Land Management publishes the relevant
data across roughly a dozen different endpoints with inconsistent
schemas, and several of the obvious filters are subtly wrong. The
chapter has the live map embedded plus a writeup of what the tool
does, how the open-land calculation is built, and what it still
doesn't catch (historic mineral patents, split estate, placer-
specific modeling).
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![goldbug live map: topo basemap, federal gap parcels (yellow), active mining claims (red), with a parcel popup showing the score breakdown and the layer-toggle panel on the right](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch3_goldbug.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/goldbug.html)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [4. Placer-Au prospectivity (California Mother Lode)](placer.qmd)

Placer modeling (hunting for gold loose in streambeds and old
gravel beds, rather than locked in solid rock) sits about a decade
behind lode prospectivity in the published literature. I built a
two-population model over the California Mother Lode at 250 m grid
spacing, training Tertiary deep-gravel and Quaternary modern-channel
placer separately because they leave different geomorphic
signatures. The latest expansion extended the Quaternary classifier
from the northern Sierra to the full Mother Lode by adding a
channel-aligned kernel along the National Hydrography Dataset
flowlines; previously near-zero southern districts now get 8 to 13
times more probability mass per cell. The calibrated raster feeds
the goldbug live tool for per-claim sampling.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![v3.6 vs v3.7.0 Quaternary placer-Au prospectivity, percentile rank against the AOI. Left: v3.6, MRDS-derived labels concentrated north of 39°N. Right: v3.7.0, USMIN + channel-aligned kernel across the full Mother Lode.](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch4_placer.png)](placer.qmd)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [5. Reproducing + auditing two published methods](reproductions.qmd)

I cloned the official code for two well-known mineral-prospectivity
papers and re-ran them with proper validation. **Lawley 2022**
(continental Zn-Pb, published area-under-the-ROC-curve of 0.983, a
standard model-quality score where 1.0 is perfect ranking and 0.5
is random) reproduces cleanly, but the published number includes a
label leak (2.4 percentage points), a spatial-blocking gap (9.0 pp),
and a cross-continent transfer collapse (around 16 pp). Properly
validated, the AUC drops to 0.71 to 0.87. The published code also
had a performance problem: a critical piece used an O(N²) algorithm
that did not finish after half an hour; I rewrote it as O(N) and
got the runtime down to a few minutes. **DEEP-SEAM** (Curnamona
rare-earth-element deposit, published 86% top-2% capture) is
measured under a non-spatial random train/test split (no spatial
blocking at all), and the implementation diverges from the paper
text in two places. I tried to find a charitable read of each and
report what I actually saw.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![Waterfall chart of Lawley 2022 AUC stages](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch5_reproductions.png)](reproductions.qmd)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [6. Cross-region experiments](cross_region.qmd)

I ran a five-region transferability test on the DEEP-SEAM
deviation-network architecture (a deep-learning approach that the
paper reports beats Random Forest by 2 to 3 times on top-k capture).
The advantage holds on its tuning dataset (Curnamona, Australia, a
rare-earth deposit with only 7 known positives) and nowhere else.
The most pointed test was on the US Western Carbonatite belt (14
positives, the same deposit type as Curnamona): DEEP-SEAM still
loses to Random Forest. On three other regions covering porphyry-Cu
and sediment-hosted Zn-Pb under spatially-blocked cross-validation,
the gap widens to 7 to 37 percentage points in favor of tree-based
methods. Useful negative result.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![DEEP-SEAM vs Random Forest across 5 regions](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch6_cross_region.png)](cross_region.qmd)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [7. Drill planning under uncertainty](drill_planning.qmd)

A prospectivity map by itself does not tell you where to put the
next drill hole. I built a POMDP planner (Partially Observable
Markov Decision Process, the math framework for sequential
decisions when the true state of the world is hidden behind noisy
observations) ported from Mern et al.'s Intelligent Prospector
(2023 + 2024) and tested it against simpler baselines. On a
synthetic stress test POMCP finds the deposit in 94% of episodes
within a 9-hole budget; the greedy baseline gets there in 53%. On
the real BC Golden Triangle data a leak-free starting prior already
captures 86% of post-2010 copper hits in its top quarter, so the
planner cannot improve on a good prior; the planning advantage
shows up against weak priors, not strong ones. Scaled up to the
full BCGT grid with real BCGS deposit-type priors, four policies
tie within experimental noise on immediate reward, but SARSOP ends
each episode about seven points more confident in the true
geological hypothesis than greedy does (0.71 vs 0.64). The chapter
walks through where multi-step planning helps, where it does not,
and what real BCGT exploration would need to look like for the
planning advantage to transfer at scale. An interactive widget at
the end lets you step a small four-cell Tiger-style problem turn
by turn.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![POMCP discovery curves on BC Golden Triangle](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch7_drill_planning.png)](drill_planning.qmd)
:::
::::

## Methodology

Keeping in mind that I am a programmer and not a statistician, here
is my take on methodology.

Federal and state surveys publish a lot of mineral data: occurrence
records, geologic maps, magnetics and gravity, stream-sediment
geochemistry, satellite imagery. I pull these onto a common grid and
each cell of land becomes a feature vector. I train a Random Forest
(an ensemble of decision trees that vote together; well-studied,
fast to fit, hard to beat on tabular geoscience data) to recognize
cells with known deposits, holding out spatial blocks so the model
can't cheat by memorizing neighbors. The output is a per-cell score
across the region: the model's guess at where the unknown deposits
are.

The part I've spent the most time on isn't the training, it's the
validation afterward. The methodology has four well-known traps:
inflated training positives, spatial leakage, exploration bias in
the labels, and cross-region transfer failure. All four show up in
this portfolio. Chapter 5 is the most explicit about quantifying
them, showing how much of two published AUC numbers (area under the
receiver-operating-characteristic curve, a standard model-quality
score for ranked predictions) each trap accounts for. The same
issues are flagged in the within-region work where I noticed them,
and I'm sure I missed some.

## Stack

Public data sources: USGS, NRCan, state surveys, Sentinel-2. Code is
Python: scikit-learn, pandas + pyarrow, geopandas + rasterio,
PyTorch (for the DEEP-SEAM port), PyVista for the Bear Cub 3D
visualization, Quarto for the site itself.

## Reproduce

```bash
git clone <this-repo>
cd ai-minerals
uv sync
quarto render
```

License: MIT.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
from datetime import date
print(f"\n---\n\n*Site rendered {date.today().isoformat()}.*")
```
